stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ 0 & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) has distinct eigenvalues, so it is diagonalizable. What is the \( (1,2) \) entry (top-right entry) of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Diagonalizing \( A = PDP^{-1} \) with \( D = \operatorname{diag}([[a]],[[d]]) \) gives \( A^{n} = \begin{bmatrix} a^{n} & b\,\frac{a^{n}-d^{n}}{a-d} \\ 0 & d^{n} \end{bmatrix} \). The \( (1,2) \) entry is \( [[b]]\cdot\frac{([[a]])^{[[n]]}-([[d]])^{[[n]]}}{[[a]]-[[d]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( (1,1) \) entry \( a^{n} \), not the \( (1,2) \) entry.
This is the \( (2,2) \) entry \( d^{n} \), not the \( (1,2) \) entry.
This forgets the factor of \( b \) from the off-diagonal entry.
This forgets to divide by \( a-d \) in the closed form.
This uses \( b\,a^{n} \), ignoring the \( d^{n} \) contribution and the denominator.
This is one more than the correct entry.
This is one less than the correct entry.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), a diagonal matrix (already its own diagonalization, with eigenvalues \( [[a]] \) and \( [[d]] \)). What is \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{[[n]]}) \)?
solution: Since \( A \) is diagonal, \( A^{n} = \operatorname{diag}([[a]]^{[[n]]}, [[d]]^{[[n]]}) \), so \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{n}) = [[a]]^{[[n]]} + [[d]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This raises the sum \( a+d \) to the \( n \)-th power instead of summing the \( n \)-th powers.
This keeps only the first eigenvalue's contribution.
This keeps only the second eigenvalue's contribution.
This multiplies the two powers instead of adding them (that would be \( \det(A^{n}) \), not the trace).
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is two less than the correct trace.
This is three more than the correct trace.

stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ 0 & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) has eigenvalues \( [[a]] \) and \( [[d]] \), so it is diagonalizable as \( A = PDP^{-1} \) with \( D = \operatorname{diag}([[a]],[[d]]) \). Using \( \det(A^{n}) = \det(D)^{n} \), what is \( \det(A^{[[n]]}) \)?
solution: Since \( \det(A) = [[a]]\cdot[[d]] \) (the product of the eigenvalues) and determinant is multiplicative, \( \det(A^{[[n]]}) = \det(A)^{[[n]]} = ([[a]]\cdot[[d]])^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the eigenvalues' powers (that would relate to a trace), instead of raising their product to the \( n \)-th power.
This multiplies \( \det(A) \) by \( n \) instead of raising it to the \( n \)-th power.
This raises the sum of the eigenvalues, not their product, to the \( n \)-th power.
This forgets to raise the second eigenvalue to the \( n \)-th power.
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is one less than the correct determinant.
This is four more than the correct determinant.

stem: The symmetric matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[b]] & [[a]] \end{bmatrix} \) has eigenvalues \( \lambda_{1} = [[a]]+[[b]] \) (eigenvector \( (1,1) \)) and \( \lambda_{2} = [[a]]-[[b]] \) (eigenvector \( (1,-1) \)). Using this diagonalization, what is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Writing \( A = PDP^{-1} \) with \( D = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2}) \) and \( P = \begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{bmatrix} \) (so \( P^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{2}P \)), the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A^{n} = PD^{n}P^{-1} \) is \( \dfrac{\lambda_{1}^{n}+\lambda_{2}^{n}}{2} = \dfrac{([[a]]+[[b]])^{[[n]]}+([[a]]-[[b]])^{[[n]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only \( \lambda_{1}^{n} \), forgetting the contribution from \( \lambda_{2} \) and the averaging by \( \tfrac{1}{2} \).
This uses only \( \lambda_{2}^{n} \), forgetting the contribution from \( \lambda_{1} \) and the averaging by \( \tfrac{1}{2} \).
This forgets to divide the sum of the two eigenvalue powers by \( 2 \).
This ignores the off-diagonal entry \( b \) entirely, as if \( A \) were diagonal.
This is one more than the correct entry.
This is two less than the correct entry.
This is three more than the correct entry.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & [[b]] & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & [[c]] \end{bmatrix} \), with distinct eigenvalues \( [[a]], [[b]], [[c]] \). What is \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{[[n]]}) \)?
distractor_rationales: This raises the sum of the eigenvalues to the \( n \)-th power instead of summing the \( n \)-th powers.
This multiplies the powers together (that is \( \det(A^{n}) \)), not the trace.
This forgets the third eigenvalue's contribution.
This forgets to raise the eigenvalues to the \( n \)-th power at all.
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is three less than the correct trace.
This is five more than the correct trace.
